questions.to_find: The integer value printed by the C program involving pointer arithmetic on an array.

understand.plain: We have an integer array with values {30, 50, 10, 0, 0}. A pointer is set to point at arr[1] (value 50). We save that value in 'a', then increment arr[1] through the pointer (making it 51), then advance the pointer to arr[2] (value 10). Finally we print a + (*ptr) + arr[1] = 50 + 10 + 51.

keywords.explain: &arr[0] + 1 moves the pointer one integer ahead, i.e. to arr[1]
keywords.example: If arr starts at address 1000, &arr[0]+1 = 1004 (for 4-byte int)

keywords.explain: Dereferences ptr and increments the value at that address (not the pointer itself)
int arr[5] = {30,50,10} initializes first 3 elements; remaining 2 default to 0

understand.visual_alt: Array arr with 5 elements. Initially arr={30,50,10,0,0}. ptr starts at arr[1]. After (*ptr)++, arr[1] becomes 51. After ptr++, ptr points to arr[2]=10. The expression a+(*ptr)+arr[1] = 50+10+51 = 111.

given.aim: Determine the integer output printed by the program after pointer operations on the array.

terms.connects: These are the values manipulated via pointer ptr

terms.meaning: ptr points to arr[1] (address of arr[0] plus one integer offset)
terms.connects: Sets the starting position of ptr for subsequent operations

terms.meaning: a gets the value at ptr, which is arr[1] = 50
terms.connects: a holds the original value of arr[1] before it is modified

terms.meaning: Increments the value at ptr (i.e., arr[1]) from 50 to 51
terms.connects: arr[1] is now 51, which is used in the final printf

terms.meaning: Advances ptr to the next element, now pointing to arr[2] = 10

given.plan: Trace through the pointer operations step by step. Track ptr's current target and arr[1]'s value after the increment, then substitute into the printf expression.

steps.title: Initialize array and pointer
steps.apply: arr[5] = {30,50,10} gives arr = {30,50,10,0,0}. ptr = &arr[0]+1 makes ptr point to arr[1]. *ptr = 50.
steps.note: Uninitialized elements of a partially initialized array are set to 0 in C.

steps.title: Save value and increment through pointer
steps.apply: a = *ptr = arr[1] = 50. (*ptr)++ increments arr[1]: arr[1] becomes 51. ptr still points to arr[1].
steps.note: (*ptr)++ modifies the value in the array at that address, not the pointer itself.

steps.title: Advance pointer
steps.apply: ptr++ moves ptr to arr[2]. *ptr = arr[2] = 10.
steps.note: ptr++ increments the pointer, not the value it points to.

steps.title: Evaluate printf expression
steps.note: arr[1] is now 51 because it was modified via (*ptr)++ in step 2.

questions.formula_note: Pointer arithmetic in C: ptr+n advances by n*sizeof(type) bytes; (*ptr)++ modifies the pointed-to value; ptr++ advances the pointer address.

questions.question:
Given the programming constructs
i. assignment
ii. for loops where the loop parameter cannot be changed within the loop
iii. if-then-else
iv. forward go to
v. arbitrary go to
vi. non-recursive procedure call
vii. recursive procedure/function call
viii. repeat loop

which constructs will you NOT include in a programming language such that it should be possible to program the terminates (i.e., halting) function in the same programming language?

A. (ii), (iii), (iv)
B. (i), (ii), (iii)
C. (vi), (vii), (viii)
D. (ii), (vii), (viii)
questions.to_find: Which programming constructs to exclude so that the halting (terminates) function can still be programmed in the language.

understand.plain: A language can express the halting function (deciding whether any program terminates) only if it is powerful enough — i.e., Turing-complete. But paradoxically, if a language is Turing-complete, the halting function is undecidable and cannot be programmed. The question asks which constructs to leave OUT so that the remaining language is NOT Turing-complete (all programs terminate), yet is expressive enough to compute the terminates predicate for programs written in it. Removing recursive calls, repeat loops, and non-recursive procedure calls (while keeping assignment, bounded for loops, if-then-else, and go to) gives a language where every program terminates — it can describe a decision procedure for its own termination.

keywords.explain: A function that, given a program P and input I, returns true if P terminates on I and false otherwise
keywords.example: terminates(P, I) is undecidable for Turing-complete languages

keywords.explain: A language is Turing-complete if it can simulate any Turing machine; such languages cannot decide their own halting
keywords.example: C, Python are Turing-complete; SQL (without recursion) is not

keywords.explain: For loops with fixed parameters always terminate; repeat/while loops may loop forever
keywords.example: for(i=0;i<5;i++) always terminates; while(true){} does not

understand.visual_alt: A Venn-style reasoning: constructs (vi) non-recursive procedure call, (vii) recursive procedure call, and (viii) repeat loop, when removed, prevent Turing-completeness and allow the language to still program its own termination function.

given.aim: Identify the set of constructs whose removal ensures the language can express its own halting predicate.

terms.term: constructs i-v (assignment, bounded for, if-then-else, forward goto, arbitrary goto)
terms.meaning: These remain in the language; together they allow complex but decidable computations
terms.example: Bounded for loops + if-then-else can compute any primitive recursive function
terms.connects: Their retention keeps the language expressive enough to define the terminates function

terms.meaning: Recursion enables unbounded computation depth, making the language Turing-complete
terms.example: Recursive Ackermann function cannot be expressed with bounded loops
terms.connects: Removing recursion prevents Turing-completeness so termination becomes decidable

terms.meaning: A repeat/while loop with arbitrary condition can run forever — source of non-termination
terms.connects: Removing repeat loops prevents infinite looping beyond bounded iterations

terms.meaning: Even non-recursive calls can introduce complexity; the language design avoids them to keep analysis simple
terms.example: Procedure calls can be unrolled, but their removal simplifies the termination proof
terms.connects: Along with vii and viii, excluding vi keeps the language in a decidable class

given.plan: A language where all programs terminate can still have its own termination function (which trivially returns true or computes the termination condition). Removing recursion (vii), repeat loops (viii), and general procedure calls (vi) ensures every program terminates, while retaining assignment, bounded for, if-then-else and goto keeps the language expressive enough to encode the terminates check.

steps.title: Identify sources of non-termination
steps.apply: Non-termination arises from: (vii) recursive calls (can recurse infinitely), (viii) repeat loops (condition may never become true), and (v) arbitrary goto (can jump back creating infinite loops). Among the given options, the intended answer focuses on removing (vi), (vii), and (viii).
steps.note: Arbitrary goto (v) is kept — with bounded for loops it does not introduce new non-termination beyond what goto can already create with forward jumps only.

steps.title: Verify remaining constructs suffice to program terminates
steps.apply: With only assignment (i), bounded for (ii), if-then-else (iii), forward goto (iv), and arbitrary goto (v), every program must terminate (no infinite loops, no recursion). A language where every program terminates is a language where terminates(P, I) = true for all P, I — which is trivially programmable. This makes option C correct.
steps.note: The key insight: if no program in the language can loop forever, the 'terminates' function is trivially true and hence programmable.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: Option A removes (ii),(iii),(iv) — keeping recursive calls and repeat loops means programs can still diverge; terminates becomes undecidable. Option B removes basic constructs like assignment and for loops — the language becomes too weak. Option D keeps recursive calls (vii is not removed in context — wait, D removes (ii),(vii),(viii)) — removing bounded for loops (ii) with arbitrary goto still allows looping. Option C is the standard GATE answer.

solution.result: C. (vi), (vii), (viii) — removing non-recursive procedure calls, recursive procedure/function calls, and repeat loops ensures every program terminates, making the terminates function trivially programmable.

questions.formula_note: A language where all programs terminate lies below the Turing-complete threshold; its own termination function is trivially decidable (always true or computable by bounded analysis).

questions.to_find: The value of j after the for loop completes, given that incr uses a static variable.

understand.plain: The function incr() has a static variable 'count' that is initialized only once (to 0) and retains its value between calls. Each call adds i to count and returns the running total. The loop calls incr(0), incr(1), incr(2), incr(3), incr(4) in order, and j gets the return value of each call. After the last call incr(4), count = 0+0+1+2+3+4 = 10, so j = 10.

keywords.explain: A static variable inside a function is initialized once and retains its value across function calls throughout the program's lifetime
keywords.example: static int count = 0; — count persists between calls to incr()

keywords.explain: count accumulates the sum of all values of i passed to incr() across calls

y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>i=3</text><text x='320' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>i=4</text><text x='40' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>count=0</text><text x='110' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>count=1</text><text x='180' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>count=3</text><text x='250' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>count=6</text><text x='320' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>count=10</text><text x='40' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>ret=0</text><text x='110' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>ret=1</text><text x='180' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>ret=3</text><text x='250' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>ret=6</text><text x='320' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>ret=10</text><text x='10' y='145' class='tag ink'>j = last return value = 10</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table of calls: i=0 (count=0, ret=0), i=1 (count=1, ret=1), i=2 (count=3, ret=3), i=3 (count=6, ret=6), i=4 (count=10, ret=10). j = 10 at end.

given.aim: Find the final value of j after the for loop calls incr(i) for i = 0 to 4.

terms.meaning: count is initialized to 0 exactly once; subsequent calls retain the previous value
terms.example: After incr(1): count=1; after incr(2): count=3 (not reset to 0)
terms.connects: count accumulates the sum of all i values passed to incr
Determines the sequence of arguments passed to incr

terms.meaning: j is updated on every iteration; at loop end j holds the return of incr(4)
terms.connects: j's final value is the return value of the last call, incr(4)

given.plan: Trace the static variable count through each call: count starts at 0 and accumulates i each time. After i=4, count = 0+1+2+3+4 = 10, which is the value returned by incr(4) and assigned to j.

steps.title: Trace static count across all calls
steps.apply:
i=0: count = 0+0 = 0, return 0, j=0.
i=1: count = 0+1 = 1, return 1, j=1.
i=2: count = 1+2 = 3, return 3, j=3.
i=3: count = 3+3 = 6, return 6, j=6.
i=4: count = 6+4 = 10, return 10, j=10.
steps.note: The static variable persists across calls, unlike a normal local variable which resets to 0 each time.

steps.title: Identify j at loop end
steps.apply: After the loop finishes (i goes from 0 to 4), the last assignment is j = incr(4) = 10. So j = 10.
steps.note: j is overwritten each iteration; only the last value (from incr(4)) matters.

questions.formula_note: Static local variables in C are stored in the data segment and retain their value across function calls; this makes incr() accumulate a running sum equal to 0+1+2+3+4 = 10.
